name: Oceanids
description: The Oceanids are the three thousand daughters of the Titans Oceanus and Tethys in Greek mythology. They are nymphs of springs, rivers, lakes, and fountains, embodying the spirit of freshwater bodies across the world. Each Oceanid was believed to be the patron of a specific body of water and possessed a unique personality reflecting the nature of her domain. They were considered benevolent spirits, often associated with healing, fertility, and the nourishment of the land. Many Oceanids were married to gods and heroes, bearing children who continued the divine lineage. Their beauty was renowned, and they were often depicted as graceful and ethereal beings. Though immortal, their influence was tied to the health and vitality of their respective waters, making them vulnerable to drought and pollution. They played a significant role in the lives of mortals, offering assistance and guidance to those who respected the natural world. They are symbols of purity, life, and the interconnectedness of all things, representing the vital role of water in sustaining life and civilization. They are often depicted attending to various gods and goddesses, especially those associated with the sea and underworld. Their numbers are vast, reflecting the countless waterways of the world, each with its own unique essence and significance. They are the guardians of freshwater, ensuring its flow and purity for all living things.
subtitle: Nymphs of freshwater springs and rivers
appearance: Oceanids are typically depicted as beautiful young women, often adorned with flowing hair and dressed in robes of shimmering blue or green. They may wear crowns of reeds or water lilies, and their skin is often described as being as smooth and cool as water. Some are portrayed with fish tails or other aquatic features, reflecting their connection to the sea. Their eyes are often described as being deep blue or green, reflecting the color of the water they inhabit. They often carry pitchers or vases from which they pour water, symbolizing their role as providers of life and nourishment.
habitat: Oceanids dwell in all bodies of freshwater, including rivers, streams, springs, lakes, and fountains. Each Oceanid is associated with a specific body of water, which serves as her home and domain.
behavior: Oceanids are generally benevolent and helpful creatures, known for their kindness and compassion. They are often willing to assist mortals who treat them with respect and offer guidance to those who seek it. However, they can also be vengeful if their waters are polluted or desecrated. They are deeply connected to the natural world and are sensitive to changes in their environment. They enjoy music, dance, and the company of other nymphs and deities.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Achilles
keyMyths.description: Thetis, an Oceanid, dipped her son Achilles in the River Styx to make him immortal, but his heel, which she held, remained vulnerable. This myth highlights the power and limitations of the Oceanids' connection to water.

keyMyths.title: Persephone's Abduction
keyMyths.description: Oceanids were among Persephone's companions when Hades abducted her, highlighting their role as attendants and friends to goddesses. This event shows their vulnerability to the actions of the gods.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Provides a comprehensive list of the Oceanids and their parentage.
sources.text: Provides a comprehensive list of the Oceanids and their parentage.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: References Oceanids in various contexts, often in relation to Thetis.
